title: 🎙️ Community Tech & Governance
description: Today we look at how Cariboo communities govern themselves and use technology for public good — from school district digital initiatives and rural broadband projects to council decisions and civic innovation. We're interested in stories where technology and local democracy intersect, and how small municipalities are solving big problems with limited resources. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we look at how Cariboo communities govern themselves and use technology for public good — from school district digital initiatives and rural broadband projects to council decisions and civic innovation. We're interested in stories where technology and local democracy intersect, and how small municipalities are solving big problems with limited resources.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Community Tech & Governance' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: municipal broadband and fiber projects in Cariboo communities, SD27 education technology and digital learning, civic tech platforms and open data initiatives, community-owned networks and ISPs, smart rural infrastructure (water, waste, transit), digital government services for rural areas, council decisions on technology spending, public Wi-Fi and connectivity programs, election technology and civic engagement platforms, rural innovation hubs, and how Williams Lake/Quesnel/100 Mile/CRD use technology for public services. Prefer articles that bridge governance AND technology in rural civic contexts. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Engadget] Toyota's still trying to make hydrogen fuel cells happen
items.content_html:
Toyota is teaming up with Daimler and Volvo to work on fuel cell technology. The Japanese company is signing on to the joint venture cellcentric that Volvo and Daimler launched back in 2020. Once it officially joins, Toyota and cellcentric will collaborate on managing the development and production of fuel cell unit cells. 

"We are deeply grateful for the opportunity to soon be joining Daimler Truck and Volvo Group as partners in building a hydrogen society," Toyota President and CEO Koji Sato said. "cellcentric which possess deep expertise in commercial fields together with Toyota ‘s over 30 years of fuel-cell development in the passenger car sector, can combine their strengths to deliver one of the world-leading fuel cell systems for heavy commercial vehicles."

It's a move that runs counter to where the auto industry has been trending. Last year, Stellantis announced that it would end its hydrogen fuel cell development program. That's the company that owns brands including Chrysler, Citroen, Fiat, Jeep and Peugeot. GM also gave up on hydrogen in 2025. Even Toyota had rethought some of its commitment to hydrogen last year, pivoting to emphasize industrial applications rather than commercial ones.

This article originally appeared on Engadget at https://www.engadget.com/transportation/toyotas-still-trying-to-make-hydrogen-fuel-cells-happen-202237728.html?src=rss

items.title: [Boing Boing] More research links artificial sweetener erythritol to stroke risk
items.content_html:
Erythritol is widely used to sweeten sugar-free foods including snacks, drinks and energy bars. A zero-calorie sugar alcohol almost as sweet as sucrose, it doesn't spike blood sugar levels and is popular with low-carb dieters. But a recent study found that it might cause blood clots in the brain, following on from earlier research that associated it with a higher risk of heart attack or stroke. — Read the rest

The post More research links artificial sweetener erythritol to stroke risk appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Audio tapes challenge Milgram obedience findings
items.content_html:
Stanley Milgram's obedience experiments, the famous 1960s studies where volunteers were told to deliver electric shocks to a stranger, have been taught in introductory psychology courses for decades as a demonstration of how readily people follow authority. Researchers David Kaposi and David Sumeghy went back to the original audio recordings, 136 of them, preserved at Yale University Library, and found something the textbook version leaves out: the "obedient" participants weren't actually obeying, according to PsyPost. — Read the rest

The post Audio tapes challenge Milgram obedience findings appeared first on Boing Boing.
